About This Workbook
Porsche 911 Technical Workbook - Reno Rennsport 2026 Edition
Published by Reno Rennsport, Reno, Nevada - 2026
This workbook is a practical technical reference for the air-cooled Porsche 911, covering model years 1965 through
1998. It is not a replacement for the Porsche factory workshop manual. It is a companion to it - a distillation of decades
of hands-on shop experience into the information you actually need when you are under the car, at the workbench, or
diagnosing a problem in the driveway.
What This Workbook Covers
The focus is the air-cooled 911 in all its production variants: the early short-wheelbase cars (1965-1968), the
long-wheelbase models (1969-1973), the G-body/impact bumper cars (1974-1989), and the 964 and 993 series
(1989-1998). Where specifications differ between US and Rest-of-World models, we note US-spec values as the
primary reference and call out ROW differences where significant.
Each chapter addresses a major system of the car:
  -  Engine - The horizontally opposed flat-six in all its displacements
  -  Fuel System - Mechanical fuel injection (CIS/K-Jetronic) and carbureted setups
  -  Ignition - Points, CDI, and electronic ignition by year
  -  Cooling - Air cooling, oil cooling, and thermal management
  -  Brakes - Calipers, rotors, master cylinders, and bleeding procedures
  -  Suspension - Torsion bars, struts, alignment, and common upgrades
  -  Electrical - Charging, starting, fuse layouts, and wiring
  -  Transmission - 4-speed, 5-speed, 915, and G50 gearboxes
  -  Maintenance - Service intervals and fluid specifications
How to Use This Workbook
Chapters are organized by system. Within each chapter you will find an overview of how the system works,
specifications organized by model year, step-by-step procedures for common jobs, and troubleshooting guides for
common failures.
Torque specifications are given in foot-pounds (ft-lbs) with Newton-meters (Nm) in parentheses. Fluid capacities are in
US quarts unless otherwise noted. Part numbers reference genuine Porsche numbers; aftermarket cross-references are
Page 2

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
noted where a commonly used alternative exists.
Diagrams and exploded views appear throughout the text. These are referenced by figure number within each chapter.
Where a procedure calls for a special tool, the Porsche tool number is listed along with commonly available alternatives.
This is a working document. Write in it. Dog-ear the pages. Keep it on your toolbox. That is what it is for.
Safety Warnings
Working on automobiles is inherently dangerous. Before performing any procedure in this workbook:
Read the entire procedure first. Do not start a job until you understand every step and have every part and tool you need
on hand.
Support the vehicle properly. Use jack stands rated for the vehicle's weight. Never work under a car supported only by a
jack. The 911's rear weight bias makes it especially prone to sliding off improperly placed stands.
Disconnect the battery before working on the electrical system or fuel system. The negative terminal comes off first; the
positive terminal goes on first during reconnection.
Relieve fuel system pressure before opening any fuel line. CIS systems maintain residual pressure even with the engine
off. Wrap fittings with a rag and wear eye protection.
Use proper ventilation. Never run the engine in an enclosed space. Carbon monoxide is odorless and lethal.
Wear eye protection when working under the car, grinding, drilling, or working with solvents.
Dispose of fluids responsibly. Used oil, coolant, and brake fluid are hazardous waste. Take them to a recycling center.
Fire safety. Keep a fire extinguisher rated for Class B (flammable liquids) within arm's reach when working on fuel
systems. The air-cooled 911 engine sits directly above the exhaust - fuel leaks in the engine bay are an immediate fire
risk.
Know your limits. If a procedure exceeds your skill level or tooling, seek professional help. A mistake on the brakes or
suspension can kill you. There is no shame in asking for guidance.
A Note from Reno Rennsport
We have been maintaining, repairing, and racing air-cooled Porsches in Reno, Nevada for many years. This workbook
reflects what we have learned - from the straightforward oil change to the full engine rebuild. Our goal is to help you
understand your car, maintain it properly, and enjoy it for decades to come.
If you find an error or have a suggestion, contact us. This workbook is a living document and we are always improving it.

Chapter 1
Chapter 1: Engine Overview
The Air-Cooled Flat-Six
The Porsche 911 engine is a horizontally opposed six-cylinder - a "flat-six" or "boxer" - mounted as a rear overhang
behind the rear axle. It is air-cooled (supplemented by an engine oil cooler) and uses a single overhead camshaft per
bank driven by a central timing chain. The crankcase is a two-piece magnesium alloy unit split vertically along the
crankshaft centerline. Cylinders are individually removable, slipped over the pistons and sealed to the crankcase with
O-rings (early) or rubber seals (later).
The fundamental architecture remained the same from 1965 to 1998, even as displacement grew from 2.0 liters to 3.6
liters and power output more than doubled.
Engine Specifications by Type
2.0L (1965-1969)
  -  Engine codes: 901/01, 901/05, 901/06, 901/08 (911S), 901/09 (911T)
  -  Bore x Stroke: 80mm x 66mm
  -  Compression: 9.0:1 (base), 9.8:1 (S)
  -  Power: 130 HP (base), 160 HP (S), 110 HP (T)
  -  Oil capacity: 9 quarts (dry fill), ~8 quarts (drain and refill with filter)
  -  Head nut torque: 23-25 ft-lbs (31-34 Nm)
2.2L (1970-1971)
  -  Engine codes: 911/03 (T), 911/06 (E), 911/01 (S)
  -  Bore x Stroke: 84mm x 66mm
  -  Compression: 8.6:1 (T), 9.1:1 (E), 9.8:1 (S)
  -  Power: 125 HP (T), 155 HP (E), 180 HP (S)
  -  Oil capacity: 9 quarts
2.4L (1972-1973)
  -  Engine codes: 911/57 (T), 911/52 (E), 911/53 (S)
  -  Bore x Stroke: 84mm x 70.4mm
  -  Compression: 7.5:1 (T), 8.0:1 (E), 8.5:1 (S)

  -  Note: 1972 models had mechanical fuel injection (MFI). Compression ratios lowered for unleaded fuel compatibility.

Common Engine Problems and Solutions
Oil leaks from valve covers. The most common 911 oil leak. Replace the rubber gaskets (Porsche P/N 930.104.911.04
or equivalent) and the acorn seal washers. Torque valve cover nuts to only 5 ft-lbs. Overtightening warps the covers and
makes the leak worse.
Cylinder head stud pull-through. On high-mileage 2.7L engines especially (the so-called "2.7 headstud problem"), the
magnesium crankcase erodes around the through-studs, allowing the head to lift and blow the head gasket. The fix is to
install case-savers (threaded inserts such as Dilavar studs) - this requires crankcase disassembly.
Chain tensioner failure. The timing chain tensioner is under constant spring and oil pressure. On cars that sit for
extended periods, the tensioner can bleed down, allowing chain slack on startup. Replace the tensioner every 60,000
miles or sooner. Use the updated hydraulic tensioner (P/N 930.105.091.04) on all 3.0 and later engines. On startup,
always let oil pressure build for a few seconds before revving.
Oil consumption. Some oil consumption is normal - up to 1 quart per 1,500 miles on an older engine is not unusual.
Excessive consumption (more than 1 quart per 500 miles) suggests worn valve guides, worn piston rings, or cracked
cylinders. A compression test and leak-down test will isolate the cause. Healthy cylinders should show 140-170 PSI
compression and less than 8% leak-down.
Exhaust stud breakage. Exhaust studs on the cylinder heads corrode and snap. Use anti-seize on installation and
replace them with high-quality studs (ARP fasteners are a popular upgrade). Broken studs must be drilled and extracted
- use a left-hand drill bit and an EZ-Out.
Camshaft chain tensioner ramp wear. Inspect the chain tensioner ramp pads for wear at every major service. Worn pads
cause chain rattle and, if left unaddressed, can allow the chain to jump timing.
Oil Specification
Use a high-quality 15W-50 or 20W-50 mineral or synthetic oil meeting API SG or SJ specification for most air-cooled
Page 7

engines. Brad Penn (PennGrade 1) 20W-50 is a popular choice. For 964 and 993 engines, Mobil 1 0W-40 or 15W-50 is
commonly used. Always use an oil with adequate zinc (ZDDP) content - at least 1,200 ppm - for the flat-tappet camshaft
in pre-964 engines.
Oil filter: Porsche P/N 911.107.620.43 (Mahle OC49) for most air-cooled engines through 3.2L. The 964/993 uses P/N
965.107.620.00 (Mahle OC234).

Chapter 2
Chapter 2: Fuel System
Overview
The majority of air-cooled 911s encountered in the US market use the Bosch CIS (Continuous Injection System), also
known as K-Jetronic. This system was standard on US-market 911s from 1974 through 1983 (2.7L and 3.0L SC
models). Earlier cars used Weber carburetors (1965-1967 non-S), Solex carburetors (911T), or Bosch mechanical fuel
injection (MFI) on 1969-1973 S and E models. Later 3.2L Carrera models (1984-1989) transitioned to L-Jetronic
electronic injection with Motronic DME, and the 964/993 used fully electronic sequential injection.
This chapter focuses primarily on the CIS K-Jetronic system, as it is the most commonly serviced fuel system in the 911
fleet.
How CIS K-Jetronic Works
K-Jetronic is a mechanical continuous fuel injection system - there are no electronic injectors and no computer. Fuel is
delivered continuously to the intake ports whenever the engine is running. The amount of fuel is controlled entirely by
mechanical and hydraulic means.
The system works as follows:
1. The fuel pump (an electric roller-cell pump located under the car, typically near the right-rear wheel) pressurizes
fuel to approximately 75 PSI (5.2 bar) system pressure.
2. The fuel accumulator (a small canister in the fuel line) dampens pressure pulsations from the pump and maintains
residual pressure after engine shutdown for hot-restart capability.
3. Fuel flows to the fuel distributor (the large casting atop the engine, containing the metering head and control
plunger). The fuel distributor sits directly on the air sensor plate housing.
4. The air sensor plate is a circular plate inside a conical venturi in the intake airbox. As the engine draws air, the
airflow lifts the sensor plate. The plate is mechanically linked to the control plunger inside the fuel distributor. As the
plunger rises, it uncovers metering slits, allowing more fuel to flow to the injectors.
5. The differential-pressure valves (one per cylinder, inside the fuel distributor) maintain a constant pressure drop
across each metering slit, ensuring fuel delivery is proportional to air sensor plate lift regardless of system pressure
variations.
6. Fuel flows from the distributor through steel and rubber lines to the injectors (one per cylinder), which are simple
spring-loaded spray nozzles. They spray continuously whenever fuel pressure exceeds their opening pressure (~3.3
Page 10

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
bar / 48 PSI).
7. Control pressure (also called counter-pressure) acts on top of the control plunger. This pressure is regulated by
the warm-up regulator (WUR), which adjusts the fuel mixture based on engine temperature. When the engine is cold,
the WUR reduces control pressure, allowing the plunger to rise more easily, enriching the mixture. As the engine
warms, an electrically heated bi-metallic strip in the WUR increases control pressure, leaning out the mixture.
Key System Pressures
Pressure
Specification
System pressure (at fuel distributor inlet)
73-78 PSI (5.0-5.4 bar)
Control pressure - cold (68degF / 20degC)
21-25 PSI (1.5-1.7 bar)
Control pressure - warm (full operating temp)
49-55 PSI (3.4-3.8 bar)
Residual (rest) pressure (5 min after shutdown)
>28 PSI (>1.9 bar)
Injector opening pressure
~47 PSI (3.3 bar)
Key Components
Fuel Pump: Bosch P/N 0 580 464 008 (commonly used replacement). Located under the car. Draws approximately 8-10
amps. Should deliver at least 750 cc/30 seconds at system pressure. Pump runs only when the engine is cranking or
running (controlled by the fuel pump relay and, on later cars, the safety inertia switch).
Fuel Filter: Bosch P/N 0 450 905 021 or Porsche 911.110.253.00. Replace every 12,000 miles. The filter is directional -
install with the arrow pointing toward the fuel distributor (direction of flow).
Fuel Accumulator: Porsche P/N 911.110.251.01. Located on the chassis near the fuel pump. If the car is hard to restart
when hot, a failed accumulator is a common cause - it allows residual pressure to bleed off.
Warm-Up Regulator (WUR): Mounted on the intake manifold to sense engine temperature. The WUR contains a
bi-metallic strip heated by engine heat and by an electric heating element (powered whenever the engine runs). A failed
WUR causes rich or lean running - typically rich when warm (control pressure too low) or lean when cold (control
pressure too high). The WUR is not rebuildable in the field; replace as a unit. Porsche P/N varies by model year -
911.606.111.00 is a common number for SC models.
Fuel Distributor and Air Sensor Plate: The fuel distributor is generally reliable. Contamination from old fuel or debris is
the primary failure mode. The air sensor plate must move freely - any binding or sticking causes severe drivability
problems. Check for free movement by gently pushing down on the plate through the airbox; it should move smoothly
with light resistance and spring back. Do not pry on the plate or bend the lever arm.
Injectors: Bosch P/N 0 437 502 007 (commonly used for 2.7/SC). Injectors should be tested for spray pattern and
leak-down. A leaking injector causes a rich cylinder, rough idle, and potential cylinder wash (diluting oil on the cylinder
wall). Replace the injector O-rings whenever the injectors are removed.

Troubleshooting Guide
Hard cold start:
  -  Check cold control pressure (should be 21-25 PSI). If too high, the WUR is not enriching.
  -  Check fuel pump delivery volume.
  -  Check for leaking injectors (fuel dripping after engine off).
Hard hot start / hot restart problems:
  -  Check residual pressure (should hold >28 PSI for at least 5 minutes after shutdown). Low residual pressure indicates
a failed check valve in the fuel pump, a leaking accumulator, or leaking injectors.
  -  Check fuel accumulator.
Rich running when warm (black smoke, sooty plugs, fuel smell):
  -  Check warm control pressure (should be 49-55 PSI). Low control pressure when warm = WUR failure.
  -  Check for stuck/binding air sensor plate.
  -  Check for leaking injectors.
Lean running / hesitation on acceleration:
  -  Check system pressure (73-78 PSI). Low system pressure = weak fuel pump or clogged filter.
  -  Check for vacuum leaks at intake boots and connections.
  -  Check that air sensor plate moves freely.
Surging at idle:
  -  Usually a vacuum leak. Check all rubber intake boots, the brake booster line, and the auxiliary air valve.
Auxiliary Air Valve: This is a supplemental idle air device mounted on the engine. It contains a bi-metallic plate that
opens a bypass air passage when cold, raising idle speed. As the engine warms, the plate closes. If the valve sticks
open, idle speed will be too high when warm. If it sticks closed, cold idle will be too low. Test by disconnecting the hoses
- idle should change when cold. Porsche P/N 911.606.101.01 (varies by year).
Fuel System Safety
CIS systems maintain residual fuel pressure even after the engine is off. Before disconnecting any fuel line:
1. Relieve pressure by loosening the fuel distributor outlet fittings slowly with a rag over the fitting.
2. Have a fire extinguisher within arm's reach.
3. Wear eye protection.
4. Do not smoke or have open flames in the work area.
Fuel hoses deteriorate over time. Replace all rubber fuel lines every 15 years or sooner if cracked, swollen, or weeping.
Use only fuel-injection-rated hose (SAE 30R9 or equivalent) - ordinary rubber hose will fail under CIS system pressures.

Chapter 3
Chapter 3: Ignition System
Overview
The air-cooled 911 used several ignition systems across its production life. Understanding which system your car uses
is the first step in diagnosing ignition problems.
  -  1965-1968: Breaker-point ignition with a Bosch mechanical/vacuum advance distributor.
  -  1969-1977: Capacitive Discharge Ignition (CDI) on most models, with breaker points used as a trigger only (not
carrying primary current). Some 911T models retained conventional points ignition.
  -  1978-1983 (SC): CDI continued, with breakerless (Hall-effect or inductive) triggering on later models.
  -  1984-1989 (3.2 Carrera): Bosch Motronic DME with integrated electronic ignition - no distributor points, no separate
CDI box. Ignition timing is computer-controlled.
  -  1989-1998 (964/993): Fully electronic ignition via Motronic DME. The 993 uses a distributorless ignition system with
individual coil packs.
Breaker-Point Ignition (1965-1968 and Some 911T Models)
The early 911 uses a conventional Bosch distributor (part number 0 231 129 022 or similar) with a single set of breaker
points and a condenser. The distributor fires all six cylinders from a single coil via the distributor cap and rotor.
Point gap: 0.35mm (0.014 in)
Dwell angle: 38deg +/- 3deg (measured with a dwell meter)
Condenser: Bosch 1 237 330 035 or equivalent
Points require regular maintenance - inspect and adjust or replace every 6,000 miles. Pitted, burned, or worn points
cause misfires, hard starting, and poor performance. When replacing points, always replace the condenser at the same
time.
Capacitive Discharge Ignition - CDI (1969-1983)
Porsche adopted CDI to improve spark energy and reduce point wear. In this system, the breaker points (or later, a
Hall-effect sensor) serve only as a trigger. The actual spark energy is produced by the CDI unit - a sealed electronic
module that charges a capacitor to approximately 300-400 volts and discharges it through the coil at the moment of
firing. This produces a much hotter spark than a conventional Kettering ignition.
Page 16

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
The CDI box is typically mounted on the left inner fender or under the rear seat area. Part numbers vary: Bosch 0 227
100 001 (early), Bosch 0 227 100 114 (later). CDI boxes are generally reliable but when they fail, the engine will not run
- there is no spark at all. They are not field-repairable; replace as a unit.
Note: On CDI-equipped cars that still use breaker points as a trigger, the points last much longer because they carry
only a few milliamps of trigger current rather than the full primary coil current. But they still wear mechanically and
should be inspected every 12,000 miles.

1 - 6 - 2 - 4 - 3 - 5

  -  Left bank (from rear): 4, 5, 6

Spark plug torque: 22-25 ft-lbs (30-34 Nm) in aluminum heads. Always apply a thin film of anti-seize to the threads. Do

Spark plug wires: Use resistive-core ignition wires (approximately 5 k? per foot) to suppress radio interference. Replace
wires if resistance exceeds 15 k? per wire or if the insulation is cracked, brittle, or oil-soaked.

ATDC = After Top Dead Center. Yes - several US-spec models are intentionally retarded past TDC at idle for emissions.
This is correct.
Timing Procedure (CDI Cars with Points Trigger, 1969-1977)
1. Warm the engine to operating temperature (oil temp at least 140degF / 60degC).
2. Connect a timing light to the #1 spark plug wire (right-rear cylinder).
3. Disconnect and plug the vacuum line to the distributor.
4. With the engine idling at the specified RPM, aim the timing light at the pulley/case timing marks on the crankshaft
pulley (visible through a hole in the engine tin on the left side of the fan housing).
5. The mark on the pulley should align with the correct degree marking on the case.
6. To adjust, loosen the distributor clamp bolt (10mm) and rotate the distributor body:
  -  Rotate counterclockwise (as viewed from above) to advance timing.
  -  Rotate clockwise to retard timing.
7. Tighten the clamp bolt and recheck. Reconnect the vacuum line.
Page 18

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
TDC mark: The crankshaft pulley has a notch or scribed line. The fan housing has degree markings: "OT" = Top Dead
Center (TDC), with marks for before (positive) and after (negative).
Common Ignition Problems
No spark - CDI cars: Check the CDI box first. Then check for 12V at the coil positive terminal with ignition on. Check the
trigger signal from the points or Hall sensor. A failed CDI box is the most common cause of total spark loss.
Intermittent misfire: Check plug wires for resistance and insulation. Check the distributor cap for carbon tracking (hairline
black lines between terminals). Replace the cap and rotor - these are maintenance items, not lifetime parts. Replace
every 24,000 miles.
Timing drift: On point-triggered cars, points wear causes timing to retard slightly over time. If timing needs frequent
adjustment, replace the points. On CDI cars with worn distributor advance mechanisms, erratic timing may indicate worn
centrifugal advance weights or springs inside the distributor.
Rotor tip burn-through: The rotor tip can erode, especially on CDI cars with their higher-voltage spark. Replace the rotor
at every cap change.

Chapter 4
Chapter 4: Cooling System
How the Air-Cooled 911 Stays Cool
The 911 has no radiator and no water pump. The engine is cooled by a combination of forced air over the cylinders and
heads, and by engine oil circulated through an external oil cooler. Understanding both systems - and how they work
together - is essential to keeping the engine alive.
Approximately 60% of engine heat rejection is through the air cooling system. The remaining 40% is handled by the oil
system. Both must be functioning properly or the engine will overheat.
Air Cooling System
A belt-driven cooling fan is mounted horizontally atop the engine, inside the fan shroud (the large black sheet-metal
housing). The fan draws air in from the engine compartment opening and forces it down through the fan shroud, where
sheet-metal ducting (engine tinwork) directs the airflow over the cylinder heads and cylinders.
The fan: On most 911s through the 3.2 Carrera, the cooling fan is an 11-blade unit driven by a V-belt from the crankshaft
pulley via the alternator/generator. The fan runs at approximately 1.3x engine speed. On the 964 and 993, the fan is
electrically driven - a significant design change.
Fan belt tension is critical. A loose belt means reduced fan speed and inadequate cooling. A belt that is too tight
accelerates bearing wear in the alternator and fan housing.
Fan belt deflection specification: 10mm (3/8 inch) of deflection at the midpoint of the longest belt span when pressed
with moderate thumb pressure (approximately 10 lbs of force).
Belt replacement: Porsche P/N 999.192.251.50 or Continental/Gates equivalent. The 911 uses a single V-belt (10 x
905mm on most models - verify for your year). Carry a spare belt in the car at all times. A broken fan belt will cause the
engine to overheat within minutes, especially in traffic or at low speed.
Engine Tinwork
The sheet-metal tinwork around the engine is not cosmetic. Every piece of tin exists to direct cooling air to a specific
area of the engine. Missing or improperly installed tin is one of the most common causes of overheating in 911s that
have been apart for service.
Page 23

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Critical pieces include:
  -  Cylinder deflector tins (between the cylinders): direct air around each cylinder barrel.
  -  Lower engine tins: seal the bottom of the engine to prevent cooling air from bypassing the cylinders.
  -  Thermostat bypass tin: works with the thermostat flaps to regulate cooling air.
  -  Oil cooler ducting: routes air through the oil cooler.
After any engine-out service, verify that all tinwork is reinstalled and properly seated. A single missing tin can cause a
localized hot spot that leads to cylinder distortion, head gasket failure, or piston seizure.
Thermostat
The air-cooled 911's thermostat is not a traditional water thermostat. It is a wax-element thermostat (mounted in the fan
shroud) that operates a set of flaps in the cooling air ducting. When the engine is cold, the flaps close, restricting cooling
airflow across the engine to bring it up to operating temperature quickly. As the engine warms, the thermostat opens the
flaps, allowing full cooling airflow.
Operating temperature: The thermostat begins to open at approximately 150degF (65degC) oil temperature and is fully
open by 185degF (85degC).
Testing: Remove the thermostat and submerge it in heated water with a thermometer. The wax element should begin to
extend at approximately 150degF. If it does not move, replace it.
Porsche P/N: 911.106.015.03 (common, verify for your year).
A stuck-closed thermostat will cause overheating. A stuck-open thermostat will prevent the engine from reaching
operating temperature, leading to increased fuel consumption, poor oil circulation, and accelerated engine wear from
running cold.
Oil Cooling
The engine oil cooler is a critical cooling component. On most models, it is a tube-and-fin cooler mounted in the
right-rear fender well, where it receives ram air through a duct in the right-rear quarter panel.
Oil flows from the engine oil pump through the oil cooler (via the oil thermostat, which regulates whether oil bypasses
the cooler when cold) and returns to the engine.
Oil thermostat: Located in the crankcase or in the oil cooler housing. Opens at approximately 185degF (85degC) to
route oil through the cooler. Below this temperature, oil bypasses the cooler and circulates only through the engine for
faster warm-up.
Oil cooler maintenance:
  -  Inspect the cooler for external damage, oil leaks, and clogged fins.
Page 24

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
  -  The air duct from the body to the cooler must be intact and unobstructed. Check the rubber seal where the duct
meets the cooler.
  -  Internally, the cooler can become restricted by sludge. If oil temperatures are chronically high despite proper fan and
thermostat function, the cooler may need to be flushed or replaced.
  -  On 2.4L and later cars with the external oil tank, also check the oil tank lines and seals.
Popular upgrade: Many owners add an auxiliary front-mounted oil cooler (Setrab, Mocal, or similar) for cars driven hard,
in traffic, or in hot climates. This is mounted behind the front bumper with a thermostatically controlled electric fan and
plumbed into the oil system.

Common Overheating Causes
Broken or loose fan belt: The number one cause of overheating field failures. The engine can overheat in as little as 2-3
minutes without the cooling fan turning. If you see the oil temperature climbing rapidly, pull over immediately and check
the belt.
Missing tinwork: Cooling air bypasses the cylinders. This causes localized overheating that may not immediately show
on the oil temperature gauge but will damage the engine over time.
Failed thermostat (stuck closed): Cooling flaps remain closed. Oil temperature climbs steadily. Replace the thermostat.
Clogged oil cooler: Oil temperature runs high despite proper air cooling. Flush or replace the cooler.
Blocked oil cooler air inlet: Debris, leaves, or rodent nests in the right-rear air intake duct. Inspect and clean annually.
Low oil level: The engine oil is a major heat transfer medium. Running low on oil reduces cooling capacity as well as
lubrication. Check the oil level before every drive - the dipstick should read between the two marks with the engine warm
and off for 5 minutes.
Prolonged low-speed driving in hot weather: The air-cooled 911 generates less cooling airflow at low RPM and low road
Page 25

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
speed. In stop-and-go traffic on a hot day, oil temperatures can climb above 250degF. If this happens, pull over and let
the engine idle in neutral with the engine cover open - or shut it off entirely and let it heat-soak. Do not continue to drive
with oil temperatures above 260degF.
Fan Belt Replacement Procedure
1. Remove the engine lid (rear deck).
2. Remove the air filter housing to access the fan shroud.
3. Loosen the alternator pivot bolt (13mm) and the adjustment bolt.
4. Push the alternator toward the engine to create belt slack.
5. Slip the old belt off the pulleys.
6. Route the new belt over the crankshaft pulley, alternator pulley, and fan pulley.
7. Pull the alternator outward to tension the belt. Check deflection: 10mm at the midpoint.
8. Tighten the alternator adjustment bolt, then the pivot bolt.
9. Recheck tension after a few minutes of running.
Belt tension that is correct when cold will loosen slightly as the belt warms up. Recheck after the first drive.

Chapter 5
Chapter 5: Brake System
Overview
The 911 uses four-wheel hydraulic disc brakes across all model years. Brake system design evolved significantly over
the car's production run, from solid rotors with single-piston calipers on the earliest cars to ventilated rotors with
four-piston aluminum calipers on later models.
The rear weight bias of the 911 (approximately 60% rear / 40% front in most configurations) means the rear brakes do
more work than in a conventional front-engine car. Proper brake balance, correct pad selection, and good maintenance
are critical for safe handling.
Brake Systems by Year
1965-1968 (A/B-series): Solid disc rotors front and rear. ATE single-piston floating calipers. No power assist on early
models.
1969-1977 (C through I-series): Solid rotors front and rear through 1974; ventilated front rotors introduced on the 1975
Turbo (930) and 1977 standard models. ATE single-piston calipers. Vacuum-assisted brake booster standard.
1978-1983 (SC): Ventilated front rotors, solid rear rotors. ATE front calipers, single-piston rears. Power-assisted brakes
standard.
1984-1989 (3.2 Carrera): Ventilated front rotors. ATE or Brembo calipers depending on market. Aluminum four-piston
fixed calipers front, two-piston rear on some late models.
1989-1994 (964): Brembo four-piston fixed calipers front, two-piston rear. Ventilated rotors front and rear. ABS standard
on most models.
1995-1998 (993): Brembo four-piston front, two-piston rear. Ventilated and cross-drilled rotors. ABS standard on all
models.

Replace rotors that are below minimum thickness or that have deep scoring, heat cracks more than 1 inch long, or
significant lateral runout (maximum TIR: 0.003 inches / 0.08mm).
Brake Pad Specifications
Use pads appropriate for the application. For street driving, a quality semi-metallic or ceramic compound is appropriate.
For track use, select a pad rated for higher temperatures (e.g., Hawk HP+, Pagid RSL or RS4-2, or Ferodo DS2500).
Common pad part numbers (street):
  -  SC front: Textar T1143 / Pagid 1143
  -  SC rear: Textar T1144 / Pagid 1144
  -  964 front: Textar T1308 / Pagid 1308
  -  964 rear: Textar T1305 / Pagid 1305
Minimum pad thickness: Replace pads when friction material reaches 2mm. Most pads include a wear indicator groove
at approximately 3mm - when the groove is no longer visible, it is time to replace.
Brake Fluid
Use DOT 4 brake fluid meeting FMVSS 116 specifications. Porsche specifies ATE Typ 200 (gold bottle) or equivalent.
For track use, a higher-boiling-point fluid such as ATE Super Blue Racing (DOT 4) or Motul RBF 600 is recommended.
Brake fluid change interval: Every 2 years or 24,000 miles, whichever comes first. Brake fluid is hygroscopic - it absorbs
moisture from the atmosphere, which lowers its boiling point and promotes internal corrosion. Old brake fluid is the most
common cause of spongy brakes and caliper piston seizure.
Brake Bleeding Procedure
The 911 uses a diagonal split hydraulic system on most models (left-front + right-rear on one circuit, right-front + left-rear
on the other). This ensures that a single hydraulic failure still provides braking to one front and one diagonally opposite
rear wheel.
Bleeding order: Right-rear ? Left-front ? Left-rear ? Right-front (farthest from master cylinder first, following each circuit).
Procedure (two-person method):
1. Fill the brake fluid reservoir to the MAX mark with fresh DOT 4 fluid. Monitor the level throughout - never let it run
dry.
Page 31

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
2. Begin with the right-rear caliper. Attach a clear hose to the bleeder valve and submerge the other end in a
container of clean fluid.
3. Have an assistant slowly press the brake pedal fully to the floor. Open the bleeder valve (8mm or 7mm wrench,
depending on year). Fluid and air will flow out.
4. Close the bleeder valve. Then have the assistant release the pedal.
5. Repeat until no air bubbles are visible in the hose and the fluid runs clear.
6. Move to the next caliper in the bleeding order.
7. After all four corners are bled, check pedal firmness. The pedal should be firm with no more than 1-1.5 inches of
free play before resistance.
8. Top off the reservoir to the MAX mark.
Bleeder valve torque: 7-10 ft-lbs (10-14 Nm). Do not overtighten - the bleeders thread into aluminum caliper bodies and
strip easily.
Tips:
  -  Use a speed bleeder (one-way check valve) or a vacuum bleeder to make this a one-person job.
  -  On ABS-equipped cars (964/993), the ABS hydraulic unit may trap air. A factory-level diagnostic tool (e.g., Porsche
System Tester or Durametric) is needed to cycle the ABS pump during bleeding.
Caliper Rebuild
Seized caliper pistons are common on 911s that sit for extended periods or have old brake fluid. Rebuilding a caliper
involves disassembling it, cleaning the piston bores, replacing all seals and dust boots, and honing the bore if lightly
scored.
Rebuild kits: Available from ATE, Brembo, and aftermarket sources. For example:
  -  SC front caliper rebuild kit: ATE P/N varies - order by caliper casting number
  -  964 front caliper seal kit: Porsche 964.352.947.02
Procedure overview:
1. Remove the caliper from the car. Disconnect the brake hose (use a line wrench to avoid rounding the fitting -
11mm is common).
2. Remove the pads and hardware.
3. Use compressed air (carefully!) to push the piston(s) out. Keep fingers clear - pistons exit with force. Place a block
of wood in the caliper to catch the piston.
4. Remove the old seal and dust boot from the caliper bore.
5. Inspect the bore for scoring or pitting. Light surface marks can be cleaned with fine Scotch-Brite or honed with a
brake cylinder hone. Deep pitting requires caliper replacement.
6. Coat new seals with clean brake fluid and install in the bore groove.
7. Coat the piston with clean brake fluid and press it into the bore by hand.
8. Install the dust boot.
9. Reassemble, install pads, remount, and bleed.
Page 32

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Master Cylinder
The tandem master cylinder is mounted on the front firewall (front trunk area), connected to the brake pedal via a
pushrod through the brake booster.
Check for: Fluid leaks at the pushrod seal or at the outlet fittings. A sinking brake pedal (pedal slowly drops when held at
steady pressure with the engine running) indicates internal master cylinder bypass - the primary or secondary piston
seals are leaking internally. Replace the master cylinder or rebuild it.
Master cylinder bore size:
  -  SC: 23.81mm
  -  3.2 Carrera: 23.81mm
  -  964: 23.81mm
  -  993: 25.4mm
Reservoir cap: The cap has a rubber diaphragm that must seal properly. If the diaphragm is torn or missing, moisture
intrusion accelerates. Replace it during every fluid change.
Brake Hoses
Rubber brake hoses should be replaced every 8-10 years, regardless of appearance. Internally, a hose can delaminate
and act as a one-way valve - allowing pressure to the caliper but not releasing it, which causes the brake to drag.
Stainless steel braided hoses (Goodridge, Spiegler, Earl's) are a popular and recommended upgrade that improves
pedal feel and eliminates hose expansion under pressure.

Chapter 6
Chapter 6: Suspension
Overview
The 911's suspension design reflects its rear-engine layout and evolved considerably across its production life. The
fundamental architecture for air-cooled models through the 1989 Carrera 3.2 uses torsion bars as the primary spring
element, front and rear. The 964 (1989-1994) and 993 (1995-1998) transitioned to coil springs with MacPherson-type
struts in front and a multi-link rear suspension.
Understanding the suspension geometry is essential for proper handling - and for diagnosing the tail-happy behavior the
911 is famous for.
Front Suspension
Early Cars Through 3.2 Carrera (1965-1989)
The front suspension is a MacPherson strut design, but instead of coil springs, it uses longitudinal torsion bars housed in
tubes that run across the front of the car. Each front wheel has:
  -  A MacPherson strut (shock absorber and steering knuckle combined)
  -  A torsion bar (connected to the lower control arm)
  -  A lower control arm
  -  A front anti-roll bar (sway bar)
The torsion bars are indexed (splined) to the lower control arm on one end and to an adjustable lever on the other. Ride
height is adjusted by repositioning the torsion bar on its splines.
Torsion bar diameter:
  -  911/911S (early): 19mm
  -  911T: 17mm
  -  SC / 3.2 Carrera: 19mm or 20mm (varies; check the parts fiche for your chassis)
Front anti-roll bar diameter:
  -  Stock varies from 15mm (early) to 20mm (SC/3.2)
  -  Common upgrades: 22mm or 24mm aftermarket bars (Weltmeister, Elephant Racing)
964 and 993 Front Suspension
Page 37

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
The 964 introduced a fully modern MacPherson strut front suspension with coil springs instead of torsion bars. The 993
refined this further with a lighter aluminum subframe and revised geometry.
Both use standard coil-over strut assemblies. Ride height adjustment requires different spring rates or adjustable spring
perches (e.g., Bilstein PSS9/PSS10, KW Variant 3).
Rear Suspension
Early Cars Through 3.2 Carrera (1965-1989)
The rear suspension uses semi-trailing arms sprung by lateral torsion bars housed in a tube that crosses the car ahead
of the rear axle. Each rear wheel has:
  -  A semi-trailing arm
  -  A torsion bar (connected to the trailing arm and indexed to the opposite side of the crossbar tube)
  -  A shock absorber (mounted separately from the spring, unlike the front)
  -  A rear anti-roll bar (on most models)
Rear torsion bar diameter:
  -  911/911S: 24mm
  -  911T: 23mm
  -  SC / 3.2 Carrera: 24mm or 25mm
Rear anti-roll bar diameter:
  -  Stock: 16mm (early) to 18mm (SC/3.2)
  -  Common upgrades: 19mm to 22mm
Note on rear torsion bar adjustment: Rear ride height is set by repositioning the torsion bar on its inner splines. This
requires removing the trailing arm - it is a significant job. Mark everything before disassembly and count splines
carefully.
964 Rear Suspension
The 964 retained a semi-trailing arm rear suspension conceptually similar to earlier cars but with coil springs instead of
torsion bars and LSA (Light, Stable, Agile) geometry changes.
993 Rear Suspension
The 993 introduced a multi-link rear suspension (referred to as "LSA" or "Weissach axle" in various literature) that
dramatically improved rear-end stability and handling. This is widely considered one of the best-handling rear
suspension setups fitted to any air-cooled Porsche.

Common Suspension Issues and Upgrades
Worn torsion bar bushings: The torsion bar pivot points use needle bearings and rubber bushings that wear over time,
causing clunking and imprecise handling. Replace with fresh OEM-spec bushings or upgraded solid/poly bushings
(Elephant Racing, Weltmeister).
Sagging ride height: Torsion bars can lose their spring rate after decades. If the car sits low and ride height cannot be
corrected with spline adjustment, the torsion bars are fatigued and should be replaced.
Worn strut mounts (front): The upper strut mount bearing allows the strut to pivot during steering. Worn mounts cause a
clunking or binding sensation. Replace the mount and bearing as a set - Porsche P/N 911.343.029.01 mount,
999.059.080.02 bearing (verify for year).
Rear trailing arm bushings: The rubber bushings in the rear trailing arm pivot wear and crack, creating slop in the rear
suspension. This is felt as vague rear-end behavior and sometimes a groaning sound over bumps. Replacement is
straightforward but requires a press.
Anti-roll bar drop links: The plastic or rubber end links that connect the anti-roll bar to the strut or control arm wear out,
causing knocking sounds over bumps. Inexpensive and easy to replace - do both sides at once.
Ride height consistency: Left-to-right ride height should be equal within 5mm. A significant difference indicates a broken
or fatigued torsion bar, improperly indexed bars, or worn bushings on one side.

Chapter 7
Chapter 7: Electrical System
Overview
The air-cooled 911 uses a 12-volt negative-ground electrical system. Early cars (1965-1973) used a Bosch
external-regulated generator (dynamo); all later cars use a Bosch internally-regulated alternator. The wiring is
straightforward by modern standards but can become a nightmare on older cars due to corroded connectors, brittle
insulation, and decades of amateur modifications.
Understanding the charging system, starting circuit, and fuse layout is essential for diagnosis.
Charging System
Generator Cars (1965-1973)
Early 911s used a Bosch 12V generator (dynamo) mounted on the engine, driven by the same V-belt as the cooling fan.
The generator output is controlled by an external Bosch voltage regulator mounted on the body.
Generator output: Approximately 420-500 watts (35-42 amps) depending on model.
Regulated voltage: 13.8-14.4V at the battery with the engine running above 2,000 RPM.
Voltage regulator: Bosch RS/TBA 160 12A2 or equivalent.
Common problems: Low charging output is often caused by worn brushes, a glazed commutator, or a faulty voltage
regulator. If the GEN/ALT warning light stays on, check the belt tension first, then the regulator, then the generator
brushes.
Alternator Cars (1974-1998)
From 1974, all 911s use a Bosch internally-regulated alternator. The alternator is mounted in the fan shroud housing
and drives the cooling fan via the V-belt.
Alternator output:
  -  1974-1983: Bosch K1 series, 770-980 watts (55-70 amps)
  -  1984-1989: Bosch N1 series, 1,050 watts (75 amps) or higher
  -  964/993: 1,260-1,540 watts (90-110 amps)
Regulated voltage: 13.8-14.4V at the battery, engine running above 2,000 RPM. Measure at the battery terminals with a
Page 44

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
multimeter - not at the alternator output post.
Alternator warning light: The dash warning light is part of the excitation circuit. If the bulb burns out, the alternator may
not charge at all. If you lose charging, check the bulb first.
Common part numbers:
  -  SC alternator: Bosch 0 120 400 724 (55 amp)
  -  3.2 Carrera: Bosch 0 120 469 643 (90 amp)
Starting System
The starting circuit consists of:
1. Battery (12V, located in the front trunk - right side on most models)
2. Ignition switch (on the left side of the steering column - a Porsche tradition since the 917 Le Mans cars)
3. Starter motor (Bosch, mounted on the bellhousing at the rear of the engine)
4. Battery cables (positive runs from the battery through the car to the starter; ground cable from the battery to the
body)
Battery specification: Group 47 (H5) or Group 48 (H6), 12V, minimum 60 Ah, CCA 500+. A quality AGM battery is
recommended (Optima Yellow Top, Odyssey PC1200, or equivalent).
Starter motor: Bosch 0 001 312 series (verify for year). The 911 starter is a pre-engaged (solenoid-actuated) type.
Common starter issues include a worn Bendix drive (starter spins but doesn't engage the flywheel ring gear) and
solenoid contact wear (clicking but no cranking).
Voltage drop test: If the starter cranks slowly, perform a voltage drop test. Measure voltage across each cable
connection while cranking. Drop should be less than 0.5V at any single connection. High resistance in the long positive
cable (which runs from the front trunk to the rear engine) is a common issue - check all connections for corrosion and
tightness.
Fuse Box
1965-1968
The fuse box is located in the left kick panel area. Eight fuses, ceramic type. These early fuse boxes are notorious for
corroded terminals. Clean terminals with fine sandpaper and apply dielectric grease.
1969-1973
Relocated to behind the left kick panel or under the dash. Twelve fuses. Still ceramic (torpedo) type.

ATO type on later models). Typically 20-26 fuses. The fuse box cover should have a diagram showing fuse numbers

The fuse box is in the left kick panel, similar location. Uses standard ATO blade fuses. Additionally, a relay board is

Relays
The 911 uses multiple relays for high-current circuits. The most important:
Fuel pump relay: Controls power to the electric fuel pump. If the engine cranks but does not start and you hear no fuel
pump whine, check the relay. On many 911s this is a combined DME/fuel-pump relay.
DME relay (3.2 Carrera and later): The "DME relay" or "main relay" powers the engine management system. A common
failure - the relay develops cracked solder joints internally from heat cycling. Symptoms: intermittent no-start, sudden
engine shutdown while driving. Porsche P/N 911.618.151.00 (often stamped "11" on the relay case). This relay can be
resoldered as a field repair.
Headlight relay: Powers the headlight circuit. A failed relay causes headlight failure.
Horn relay: If the horn doesn't work, check the relay and the horn ground before condemning the horn itself.
Common Electrical Problems
Page 46

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Intermittent no-start (3.2 / 964): The DME relay (see above) is the first suspect. Pull the relay, open the case, and
inspect for cracked solder joints on the circuit board. Resolder or replace.
Dim headlights / poor grounds: The 911's body-grounding system relies on multiple ground points that corrode over
time, especially in the front trunk (battery area) and at the engine grounding strap. Clean all ground connections with
sandpaper and apply dielectric grease. Add supplemental chassis-to-engine ground straps if necessary.
Flickering or dead instruments: Check the voltage regulator for the instrument cluster (a small voltage stabilizer that
maintains 10V for the gauges). Also check the printed circuit board behind the instrument cluster - cracked traces are
common.
Inoperative fuel gauge: Often caused by a failed sending unit in the fuel tank rather than the gauge itself. The sending
unit is a float-operated variable resistor. Test by disconnecting the sender wire and grounding it - the gauge should read
full. If it does, the sender is bad.
Melted wiring at ignition switch: The ignition switch on 911s carries substantial current. Over time the switch contacts
can burn, causing resistance and heat buildup. The wiring harness connector at the back of the switch melts. Inspect
and replace the switch and connector if damaged.
Wiring Tips
  -  Use proper connectors. Soldered and heat-shrink-insulated connections are preferred. Avoid wire nuts and electrical
tape.
  -  Route wires away from exhaust. The air-cooled 911 engine bay is hot. Use high-temperature wire (e.g., TXL or GXL
automotive wire) and protect with braided loom or fiberglass sleeving near the exhaust.
  -  Label everything. If you add circuits or modify wiring, label every wire at both ends.
  -  Carry spare fuses. Keep an assortment of blade fuses in the glovebox.
  -  Wiring diagrams: Obtain the Porsche factory wiring diagram for your specific year. The diagrams in the Haynes or
Bentley manuals are often simplified. The factory electrical manual (Porsche workshop manual, Volume IV - Wiring
Diagrams) is the definitive source.

Chapter 8
Chapter 8: Transmission
Overview
The air-cooled 911 used several manual transmission types during its production run. All are rear-mounted transaxles
(combined transmission and differential) bolted directly to the engine. Understanding which gearbox your car has is
important, as they differ significantly in parts, service procedures, and fluid requirements.
There was never a factory automatic option on the air-cooled 911 (the Sportomatic semi-automatic, offered 1968-1980,
is a torque-converter-assisted manual and has its own service requirements not covered here).
Transmission Types
901/911 4-Speed and 5-Speed (1965-1971)
The original 911 transmission, designated Type 901 (later called 911 in production). Available in 4-speed and 5-speed
configurations. These are fully synchronized dog-ring transmissions with a side-shift mechanism.
  -  4-speed: Standard on 911T through 1968.
  -  5-speed: Standard on 911S and most 911/911E models.
  -  Gear oil: 80W-90 GL-4 gear oil. Do not use GL-5 - the extreme-pressure additives in GL-5 oil attack the bronze
synchro rings in these transmissions.
  -  Oil capacity: Approximately 2.5-3.0 liters (2.6-3.2 US quarts).
Type 915 5-Speed (1972-1986)
Introduced for the 1972 model year, the Type 915 replaced the earlier 901 gearbox. It is a stronger, redesigned unit with
an improved shift mechanism. The 915 was used through the 1986 model year (overlapping with the G50's introduction
in 1987).
  -  Shift pattern: Standard H-pattern, 5-speed. First gear is to the left and forward (the "dog leg" first gear on some
versions). The 1976+ US-spec pattern has 1st at the standard upper-left position.
  -  Gear oil: SAE 80W-90 GL-4 hypoid gear oil. Again, GL-4 only, not GL-5. Recommended: Swepco 201 80W-90,
Redline MT-90, or Motul Gear 300 (75W-90, GL-4/GL-5 compatible with synchro-safe formulation).
  -  Oil capacity: 3.0 liters (3.2 US quarts).
  -  Oil drain plug torque: 37 ft-lbs (50 Nm).
  -  Oil fill plug torque: 37 ft-lbs (50 Nm).
Page 51

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Known 915 issues:
  -  Second gear synchro wear. The 915's second-gear synchro is its weak point. Aggressive downshifting or insufficient
warm-up accelerates wear. Symptoms: grinding or resistance when shifting into second, especially when cold. Solution:
double-clutch on downshifts, use proper GL-4 gear oil, and replace the synchro when it becomes obstructive.
  -  Shift rod joint wear. The shift linkage connects the gearshift lever (in the center tunnel) to the transmission via a shift
rod with a joint (the "shift coupler" or "hockey puck"). This rubber-and-metal joint wears out, causing sloppy, imprecise
shifts. Replace with the updated Porsche joint or a delrin/solid aftermarket version. Porsche P/N 911.424.237.01.
Type G50 5-Speed (1987-1998)
The G50 is a vastly improved transmission introduced in the 1987 Carrera 3.2 and used through the end of air-cooled
production (993). It features a Borg-Warner synchro design (instead of the Porsche dog-ring system), a hydraulic clutch
(replacing the cable clutch of the 915), and greatly improved shift quality.
  -  Gear oil: Use Porsche-specified gear oil or equivalent 75W-90 GL-4/GL-5 synthetic. Mobil Synthetic Gear Oil
75W-90, Redline 75W-90NS (No Slip - for limited-slip-equipped cars), or Swepco 201.
  -  Oil capacity: 3.25 liters (3.4 US quarts).
  -  Oil drain plug torque: 37 ft-lbs (50 Nm).
  -  Oil fill plug torque: 37 ft-lbs (50 Nm).
G50 notes:
  -  The G50 is significantly more robust than the 915 and tolerates abuse better.
  -  964 and 993 G50 variants differ in ratios and internals but share the basic architecture.
  -  Limited-Slip Differential (LSD): Available as an option on many G50-equipped cars. The factory LSD is a ZF
clutch-pack type, with 20-40% locking. Change the LSD oil at the same interval as the gear oil. Use a
limited-slip-specific additive or NS-type oil.
Clutch System
Cable Clutch (Type 901 and 915 - 1965-1986)
The clutch is operated by a cable running through the center tunnel from the pedal to a release lever on the
transmission. Cable adjustment is required as the clutch disc wears.
Clutch cable adjustment (915):
1. Locate the adjustment nut at the transmission end of the cable (at the clutch release lever).
2. Loosen the locknut (13mm).
3. Adjust the cable so that the clutch release lever has approximately 15-20mm (5/8 inch) of free play at the lever
before the clutch begins to disengage.
4. At the pedal, this translates to approximately 15-25mm (5/8 to 1 inch) of free play before you feel resistance.
5. Tighten the locknut.
Page 52

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
6. Test by starting the engine in neutral, pressing the clutch fully, and shifting into first. The shift should be smooth
with no grinding. If the car creeps forward with the clutch fully depressed, the cable needs more free play (clutch not
fully disengaging).
Clutch cable P/N: 911.423.721.04 (verify for year).
Hydraulic Clutch (G50 - 1987-1998)
The G50 uses a hydraulic clutch actuated by a master cylinder (at the pedal) and a slave cylinder (on the transmission
bellhousing), connected by a hard line and flexible hose.
The hydraulic clutch is self-adjusting - there is no cable adjustment. However, the hydraulic system requires bleeding
(same procedure as brakes) if the clutch pedal feels spongy or the clutch does not fully disengage.
Clutch fluid: DOT 4 brake fluid (shares the same fluid as the brake system on some models, or has a separate reservoir
on others - check your model).
Clutch master cylinder P/N: 964.423.012.00 (964).
Clutch slave cylinder P/N: 964.116.414.00 (964, verify for year).
Clutch Disc and Pressure Plate
The 911 uses a single dry-plate clutch, with a diaphragm-spring pressure plate (Sachs/ZF).
Disc diameter:
  -  901/915: 225mm (8.86 in)
  -  G50: 240mm (9.45 in)
Minimum disc lining thickness: 1.5mm (riveted). Replace if near rivets.
Clutch kit (disc + pressure plate + release bearing):
  -  915: Sachs K70065-01 or equivalent.
  -  G50 (3.2): Sachs K70096-01 or equivalent.
  -  G50 (964): Sachs K70316-01 or equivalent.
Flywheel bolts (pressure plate to flywheel): Torque to 18 ft-lbs (24 Nm). Use threadlocker (Loctite 242 or equivalent).
Replace stretched bolts.
Shift Linkage
915 Shift Linkage
Page 53

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
The 915 shift linkage runs through the center tunnel as a rigid rod with a joint at each end. The front joint (under the shift
lever) and the rear joint (at the transmission input) are the primary wear points.
Shift coupler ("hockey puck"): A rubber-bonded-to-metal joint at the rear of the shift rod. When worn, the shift lever has
excessive freeplay and shifts feel vague. This is the single most common "sloppy shift" fix on a 915-equipped car.
Replacement is straightforward:
1. Raise the car and support on jack stands.
2. Locate the shift coupler at the front of the transmission (visible from below, near the shift rod).
3. Remove the two 13mm bolts connecting the coupler halves.
4. Replace the coupler. Porsche P/N 911.424.237.01. Aftermarket solid delrin couplers are available for a firmer shift
feel.
5. Reassemble and test.
G50 Shift Linkage
The G50 uses a similar rod-and-joint arrangement but with improved bushings. The shift feel is inherently better but
bushings still wear. The shift joint (sometimes called the "shift rod joint" or "carrier bearing") should be inspected and
replaced if worn. Selection of gears (left-right movement) is controlled by a separate mechanism.
Differential Oil Change
On all models, the differential shares the transmission housing and uses the same oil. There is no separate differential
oil change - changing the transmission gear oil services both.
Procedure:
1. Drive the car to warm the oil (warm oil flows faster and carries more contaminants out).
2. Raise the car on jack stands, level.
3. Place a drain pan under the transmission.
4. Remove the drain plug (Allen or hex head, varies by model). Let the oil drain completely.
5. Inspect the magnetic drain plug for excessive metal - fine paste is normal; chips or chunks indicate a problem.
6. Replace the drain plug washer (aluminum crush washer). Torque to 37 ft-lbs.
7. Remove the fill plug (located higher on the case, on the side).
8. Fill with the correct oil until it begins to seep from the fill hole. This ensures the correct level.
9. Replace the fill plug with a new crush washer. Torque to 37 ft-lbs.
10. Drive the car and recheck for leaks.

Chapter 9
Chapter 9: Maintenance Schedule
Overview
Regular maintenance is the single most important thing you can do for the longevity of your air-cooled 911. The engine,
in particular, is precision-machined from magnesium and aluminum alloys that do not tolerate neglect. Oil quality, valve
adjustment, and ignition timing are not optional - they are the price of admission for keeping these engines alive.
The following schedule is based on Porsche factory recommendations, adjusted for real-world experience. We organize
it by mileage interval. If the car is driven infrequently, use time-based intervals as well - change the oil at least once a
year regardless of mileage.
Every 3,000 Miles (or Every 3 Months)
This is the basic oil change interval. Modern synthetic oils can arguably go longer, but the air-cooled 911 engine runs
hotter and harder than a water-cooled engine, and oil degrades faster. For engines with flat-tappet camshafts (all
pre-964), frequent oil changes with high-ZDDP oil are essential to prevent cam lobe and lifter wear.
Engine oil change:
  -  Drain the oil with the engine warm (5 minutes after shutdown).
  -  Oil drain plug: 21mm hex. Torque to 37 ft-lbs (50 Nm) with a new aluminum crush washer (Porsche P/N
900.123.106.30).
  -  Replace the oil filter.
  -  Through 3.2 Carrera: Porsche P/N 911.107.620.43 (Mahle OC49)
  -  964/993: Porsche P/N 965.107.620.00 (Mahle OC234)
  -  Oil filter cover bolt torque: 7 ft-lbs (10 Nm). Do not overtighten.
  -  Refill quantity: see Chapter 1 for your engine. Typical 3.0-3.2L engine: approximately 10-11.5 quarts total capacity; a
drain-and-refill with filter change uses approximately 8-9 quarts.
  -  Recommended oil: Brad Penn (PennGrade 1) 20W-50 or equivalent with ZDDP content above 1,200 ppm for
flat-tappet engines. For 964/993: Mobil 1 0W-40 or 15W-50 synthetic.
Check oil level on the dipstick after filling, running the engine for 30 seconds, shutting off, and waiting 5 minutes. Level
should be between the marks.
Inspect for obvious leaks (valve covers, oil filter housing, oil cooler lines, oil return tubes).
Check tire pressures:
  -  Front: 29 PSI cold (street driving)

  -  Adjust for load, temperature, and track use per Porsche recommendations.

Valve adjustment:
Valve lash on the air-cooled 911 must be checked and adjusted regularly. The screw-and-locknut adjusters on the
rocker arms do not have hydraulic compensation - clearance must be set manually.

  -  Turn the engine to TDC on the cylinder being adjusted (both valves closed - no resistance on either rocker arm).
  -  Using a feeler gauge, check the gap between the rocker arm and the valve stem.

Replace spark plugs. Use the correct plug type and gap per Chapter 3. Torque to 22-25 ft-lbs (30-34 Nm) with

Replace fuel filter: Porsche P/N 911.110.253.00 (or Bosch 0 450 905 021). Relieve fuel pressure before removal.

Inspect ignition points (if equipped): Replace if pitted or worn. Replace the condenser at the same time. Adjust dwell

Check all rubber fuel lines for cracking, swelling, or weeping. Replace any suspect lines with fuel-injection-rated hose.
Inspect CV joint boots (if applicable): On 964/993, check the CV joint boots for tears. A torn boot is an inexpensive fix; a

  -  Type 915 and earlier: 80W-90 GL-4. Capacity: 3.0-3.2 quarts.
  -  G50: 75W-90 synthetic (GL-4/5 synchro-safe). Capacity: 3.4 quarts.
  -  Drain with oil warm. Replace drain plug crush washer. Torque: 37 ft-lbs.
  -  Fill to the bottom of the fill hole.
Inspect engine mounts: Check the rubber transmission and engine mounts for cracking, sagging, or separation. Worn
mounts allow the engine to shift, which can cause shift linkage misalignment and driveline vibration.
Every 24,000 Miles (or Every 2 Years)
Everything in the 12,000-mile service, plus:
Replace brake fluid: Complete flush. Bleed all four corners per Chapter 5 procedure. Use DOT 4 (ATE Typ 200 or
equivalent).
Replace clutch fluid (G50 hydraulic clutch cars): Bleed the clutch slave cylinder using the same DOT 4 fluid.
Replace distributor cap and rotor (if not done at 12K).
Inspect timing chain and tensioner: On engines with mechanical chain tensioners, inspect the chain for stretch and the
tensioner ramp for wear. Replace the tensioner if there is any question. On 3.0 and later engines, the updated hydraulic
tensioner (P/N 930.105.091.04) should be installed if not already present.

Compression test and leak-down test:
  -  Compression: all cylinders should read within 15 PSI of each other. Typical healthy readings: 145-175 PSI depending
on engine and compression ratio.
  -  Leak-down: less than 8% is excellent; 8-15% is acceptable; above 15% indicates a problem (rings, valves, or head
gasket).
Inspect spark plug wires: Measure resistance with an ohmmeter. Replace if any wire exceeds 15 k? or insulation is
damaged.
Inspect suspension bushings: Check all rubber suspension bushings for cracking and deterioration. Focus on front

Inspect brake hoses: Check all four flexible brake hoses for cracking, bulging, or chafing. Replace if older than 10 years

Inspect exhaust system: Check for leaks at the header flanges and at the muffler connections. Tighten loose clamps.
Replace rusted or cracked heat exchangers (these also provide cabin heat - a leaking heat exchanger can allow exhaust

A Final Note
The air-cooled 911 rewards consistent, attentive maintenance. These are not "set it and forget it" engines. They were
designed in an era when regular service was expected, and they have no patience for neglect. Follow this schedule, use
quality parts and fluids, and keep records. A well-documented maintenance history is worth thousands of dollars at
resale - and more importantly, it keeps the engine alive and you on the road.

Chapter 10
Chapter 10: Complete Engine Rebuild Guide
Reno Rennsport Air-Cooled Technical Workbook
When Does Your Engine Need a Rebuild?
Every air-cooled Porsche engine will eventually need rebuilding. The question is not if, but when, and whether you catch
it before catastrophic failure. Here are the diagnostic indicators that tell you it is time.
Compression Testing
A leak-down test is far more informative than a simple compression test for air-cooled engines. On a healthy engine,
expect 3-5% leakage. At 8-10%, the engine is tired but serviceable. At 15%+, a rebuild is due. Where the air leaks tells
you what is worn: air hissing from the oil filler means rings or cylinder walls; from the intake or exhaust means valves;
from the adjacent cylinder means a head gasket or deck surface issue.
For a standard compression test, healthy readings vary by engine:
  -  2.0L (901/01 engine): 128-156 PSI
  -  2.2L (911/01-911/08): 142-156 PSI
  -  2.4L (911/52-911/57): 142-157 PSI
  -  2.7L (911/83): 142-156 PSI
  -  3.0 SC (930/04-930/09): 142-171 PSI
  -  3.2 Carrera (930/20-930/25): 157-171 PSI
  -  3.6 (M64/01-M64/05): 171-185 PSI
More important than absolute numbers is variation between cylinders. More than 15 PSI spread across cylinders
indicates a problem.
Oil Consumption
New or freshly rebuilt air-cooled engines consume some oil by design -- the cylinders have no water jacket and thermal
expansion is managed partly through oil. Consumption of 1 quart per 2,000-3,000 miles is normal for most engines. At 1
quart per 1,000 miles, plan a rebuild. At 1 quart per 500 miles, stop driving and get on it before bearing damage occurs
from low oil volume.
Page 63

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Bearing Noise
A deep knocking sound at idle that increases with RPM points to rod bearing failure. A rumble felt through the case at
low RPM suggests main bearings. Either condition requires immediate shutdown. A metallic rattle at startup that fades
after 5-10 seconds is often the timing chain tensioner or guides, which can be addressed without a full rebuild on some
engines but warrants investigation.
Top End Rebuild vs. Full Rebuild
A top end rebuild involves removing the cylinders and heads while leaving the crankcase assembled. This addresses
worn cylinders, pistons, rings, valve guides, valve seats, and valve springs. It is appropriate when bottom-end bearing
clearances are within spec, the crankshaft journals are not scored, and there is no case damage.
A full rebuild means splitting the crankcase halves and replacing main bearings, rod bearings, inspecting or replacing
the crankshaft, cam, lifters, and oil pump. If the engine has more than 100,000 miles, if bearing noise has been present,
or if the case halves have separated before, do a full rebuild. False economy on a partial rebuild often leads to a second
teardown within 20,000 miles.
Disassembly Procedure Overview
Remove the engine from the car (see your model-specific manual for engine drop procedure). Begin by removing all
accessories: alternator/generator, intake manifold, heat exchangers, exhaust, shrouding, and tin. Remove the fan
housing and fan. Unbolt the heads from the cylinders (loosen head nuts in a star pattern to prevent warping). Pull the
cylinders off the pistons. Remove the piston pin circlips and press out the wrist pins. Remove the rocker arms and
shafts. Pull the cam followers (lifters).
For a full rebuild, remove the flywheel (impact wrench on the gland nut or use Porsche tool P212), the timing chain
cover, chain, and sprockets. Remove the intermediate shaft. Unbolt the rod caps and push pistons/rods out through the
top. Remove the case through-bolts or studs, then carefully separate the case halves on a clean bench. The crankshaft
will lift out with the main bearings.
Critical: Bag and label every fastener. Photograph everything. Air-cooled Porsche engines have many length-specific
fasteners that look identical but are not interchangeable.
Measuring Cylinder Bores
Use an inside micrometer or dial bore gauge. Measure each cylinder at three heights (top of ring travel, middle, and
bottom) and at two orientations 90 degrees apart. This gives you taper and out-of-round readings.
Page 64

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
  -  Taper limit: 0.05mm (0.002 in) maximum. Taper beyond this causes ring seal failure and oil consumption.
  -  Out-of-round limit: 0.03mm (0.001 in) maximum.
If either limit is exceeded, the cylinders must be replaced (Nikasil-coated and Alusil) or bored and honed (iron liners). On
slip-in cylinder engines (2.0 through 2.7), new cylinders are matched to the case and pistons as a set -- you cannot
simply swap one cylinder.
Piston and Cylinder Selection
Mahle Pistons
Mahle has been the OEM piston supplier for Porsche since the beginning. Aftermarket alternatives exist from companies
like JE, Wiseco, and CP-Carrillo, but for a street rebuild, Mahle remains the gold standard for thermal expansion
characteristics, ring seal, and longevity. Mahle P/N sets are available for all displacement variants.
Cylinder Types
  -  Biral (iron liner cast into aluminum fin pack): Used on early 2.0L engines. Reliable and rebuildable. Can be bored
oversize (+0.25, +0.50, +0.75, +1.0mm).
  -  Nikasil (nickel-silicon carbide plating on aluminum): Used from 2.7L RS onward and standard on 3.0 SC, 3.2 Carrera,
and 3.6. Extremely hard surface. Cannot be conventionally bored -- requires replating if worn. Nikasil cylinders are
sensitive to high-sulfur fuel, which caused widespread bore scoring in some markets.
  -  Alusil (aluminum-silicon alloy, no liner): Used on some 2.7L and later engines. The silicon crystals in the alloy form
the bearing surface after chemical etching. Durable, but if the bore is damaged, the cylinder must be replaced or
sleeved.
For most rebuilds, a matched piston-and-cylinder set from Mahle (or equivalent) sized for your engine is the correct
approach. Individual replacement of one cylinder is possible but demands careful measurement to ensure bore diameter
consistency across all six.
Crankshaft Inspection and Bearing Clearances
Have the crankshaft Magnafluxed to check for cracks. Inspect journal surfaces with a micrometer. Porsche crankshafts
are forged steel and extremely durable, but can be damaged by bearing failure.
Target bearing clearances:
  -  Main bearing journals: 0.03-0.06mm (0.001-0.0024 in) oil clearance
  -  Rod bearing journals: 0.03-0.06mm (0.001-0.0024 in) oil clearance
Page 65

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
  -  End play (crankshaft thrust): 0.07-0.13mm (0.003-0.005 in)
Bearings are available in standard and undersizes (-0.25, -0.50, -0.75mm). Rod bearings are tri-metal (copper-lead-tin
overlay). Use Plastigage during assembly to verify clearances. If journals are scored beyond 0.025mm depth, the crank
must be reground to the next undersize.
Camshaft Specs and Timing Chain
Porsche air-cooled engines use a single camshaft in the crankcase driven by a duplex (double-row) timing chain. The
chain rides on fixed guides and a hydraulic tensioner. Chain stretch is a common issue above 80,000 miles.
Cam lobe lift (stock profiles):
  -  2.0L 911S: Intake 10.4mm, Exhaust 10.15mm
  -  2.7L CIS: Intake 10.0mm, Exhaust 10.0mm
  -  3.2 Carrera: Intake 10.6mm, Exhaust 10.3mm
  -  3.6 (964): Intake 10.8mm, Exhaust 10.5mm
Cam lobe wear limit: 0.10mm below nominal indicates replacement. Check with a micrometer across each lobe and
compare to base circle.
The timing chain should be replaced at every rebuild regardless of apparent condition. Use only the genuine IWIS
duplex chain (Porsche P/N 930 105 911 01 or equivalent for your engine). Replace the tensioner pad, all chain guides,
and the tensioner piston seal.
Cam timing: Set TDC on cylinder #1 using the flywheel mark. Align the cam sprocket dot with the case seam. The
intermediate shaft drive gear dot aligns with its corresponding mark. Verify with a degree wheel and dial indicator on #1
intake lifter.
Head Rebuilding
Valve Guides
Check valve-to-guide clearance with a dial indicator at the valve head. Maximum allowable: 0.10mm intake, 0.13mm
exhaust. Beyond this, oil is drawn down the guides into the combustion chamber. Guides are pressed interference fit --
heat the head to 120C (250F) and press old guides out, new guides in. Porsche factory guides are phosphor bronze
(intake) and cast iron (exhaust). Many rebuilders now use manganese bronze for both.
Valve Seats
Page 66

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Machine a three-angle valve job: 30-degree top cut, 45-degree seat, 60-degree throat. Seat width should be 1.5-2.0mm
for both intake and exhaust. Hardened exhaust seat inserts are available for running on unleaded fuel if your heads
predate 1976.
Valve Springs
Check free length and compare to spec (varies by engine). Spring pressure at installed height is critical: too low causes
valve float; too high accelerates cam wear. For 3.2 Carrera: intake spring free length 42.5mm, installed height 34.5mm,
seated pressure approximately 35 kg (77 lbs). Replace all springs as a set.
Assembly Torque Specifications
These are critical values. Use a calibrated torque wrench. All fasteners should be lightly oiled unless noted otherwise.
Case Fasteners
  -  Case through-bolts (M8): 25 Nm (18 ft-lbs)
  -  Case through-bolts (M10): 35 Nm (26 ft-lbs)
  -  Case through-bolts (M12): 50 Nm (37 ft-lbs)
  -  Case perimeter bolts (M8): 25 Nm (18 ft-lbs)
Head and Cylinder Fasteners
  -  Head studs/nuts (M10 x 1.25): 34 Nm (25 ft-lbs) -- this is critical. Tighten in star pattern, in three stages.
  -  Head studs/nuts (M8): 25 Nm (18 ft-lbs)
  -  Cylinder base sealing (when applicable): ensure cylinders are seated against the case spigot with no gaps.
Rotating Assembly
  -  Rod bolt nuts (standard): 40 Nm (29 ft-lbs) -- some sources specify 38-42 Nm. Use rod bolt stretch method if possible
(0.15-0.18mm stretch).
  -  Main bearing studs (Dilavar, M12): 50-55 Nm (37-41 ft-lbs)
  -  Through-bolt main studs (M14): 80 Nm (59 ft-lbs)
  -  Flywheel gland nut: 300-350 Nm (221-258 ft-lbs) -- always use a new lock plate.
Valvetrain
  -  Rocker arm shaft nuts: 20 Nm (15 ft-lbs)
  -  Valve adjusting screw locknut: 12-15 Nm (9-11 ft-lbs)

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
  -  Cam sprocket bolt: 25 Nm (18 ft-lbs) with locking tab washer
Break-In Procedure
The first 1,500 miles are the most important miles in your rebuilt engine's life. Follow this carefully:
1. First start: Prime the oil system by cranking the engine with the coil wire disconnected until the oil pressure light
goes out (10-15 seconds). Then start the engine and immediately bring it to 2,000 RPM. Do NOT let it idle. Maintain
2,000-2,500 RPM for 15-20 minutes. This seats the cam lobes against the lifters -- insufficient RPM during initial
break-in is the #1 cause of cam lobe failure.
2. First 500 miles: Vary RPM constantly between 2,000 and 4,500. Never hold a steady RPM for more than 30
seconds. No full throttle. No lugging below 2,000 RPM. Use conventional (non-synthetic) oil -- typically 15W-40 or
20W-50 mineral oil.
3. 500-1,500 miles: Gradually extend the RPM range up to 5,500. Short bursts of moderate acceleration help ring
seating. Continue to avoid steady-state driving.
4. At 1,500 miles: Change oil and filter. Re-torque head nuts. Adjust valves. Inspect for leaks. Switch to your
preferred oil (now synthetic is acceptable).
Engine-Specific Notes
2.0L (1965-1969)
The original 911 engine. Bore 80mm, stroke 66mm. Type 901/01 (130 HP 911S) through 901/14. Crankshaft is a 65mm
main journal, 8-dowel design. Cam chain tensioner is the early hydraulic type -- these can leak down and allow chain
slap on cold starts. Cylinder base sealing is a known weak point; use Permatex Aviation Form-A-Gasket on the spigot.
2.2L (1970-1971)
Bore increased to 84mm, stroke 66mm. Improved oiling system with larger oil pump. The 2.2S uses mechanical fuel
injection (MFI). Type 911/01 through 911/08 engines. A reliable platform. Head stud pattern and case design are very
similar to 2.0.
2.4L (1972-1973)
Stroke increased to 70.4mm. First year of external oil filler on the right fender (1972, moved back to engine for 1973).
CIS fuel injection appears on US-spec 911T (911/57). Improved oil cooler and thermostat system. Magnesium
crankcase introduced on some models -- see notes on mag case repairs below.
Page 68

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
2.7L (1974-1977)
Bore 90mm, stroke 70.4mm. The most problematic of the air-cooled engines due to the head stud tensile strength issue.
The 2.7L uses 10mm head studs that are the same size as the smaller displacement engines, despite higher cylinder
pressures. Porsche specified Dilavar studs (a nickel-iron-cobalt alloy with very low thermal expansion) but the tensile
strength of these studs is lower than steel. Head stud pull-out from the magnesium case is the most common
catastrophic failure mode. Solution: Use case savers (threaded steel or stainless inserts, e.g., from Raceware or Clewett
Engineering) installed into the case stud bosses. Pair with high-quality aftermarket steel studs. If your 2.7 has never had
case savers, do it at every rebuild as preventive maintenance.
The 2.7 also introduced the thermal reactor exhaust system for emissions, which creates enormous heat in the engine
compartment and accelerates component degradation.
3.0 SC (1978-1983)
Bore 95mm, stroke 70.4mm. Bosch K-Jetronic CIS. Engine types 930/04 through 930/17. A major improvement over the
2.7. Larger head studs (some applications), Nikasil cylinders standard. The SC is one of the most durable air-cooled
engines. Aluminum crankcase with Dilavar through-bolt main bearing attachment. Oil squirters added for piston cooling.
3.2 Carrera (1984-1989)
Bore 95mm, stroke 74.4mm. DME engine management (Motronic). Engine types 930/20 through 930/25. Higher
compression (10.3:1). Updated cam profile for better mid-range torque. Chain tensioner is the improved ramp-type
design. An excellent engine; most rebuilds at this displacement are straightforward. Factory rated at 207-231 HP
depending on market.
3.6L (1989-1994, 964)
Bore 100mm, stroke 76.4mm. Dual-plug ignition, twin-spark distributors. Engine type M64/01-M64/05. Motronic DME 5.x
management. These engines are generally very robust but prone to cylinder liner separation from the case if
overheated. Always inspect the cylinder spigot sealing surfaces. The 3.6 uses hydraulic chain tensioners -- these are
maintenance items and should be replaced at every rebuild with the updated dual-row unit.
Known Issues and Debates
Magnesium Case Repairs
Magnesium crankcases (standard on 2.4 and later) are lighter but more fragile than the aluminum cases used on 2.0
and 2.2 engines. Stud bosses can crack, thread inserts can pull, and impact damage is common. Magnesium can be
Page 69

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
TIG welded by a specialist, but the heat-affected zone must be carefully managed. Lock-N-Stitch is a cold repair method
for cracks. For stud boss repair, Heli-Coil or Time-Sert inserts are used, but purpose-built case savers (Clewett,
Raceware, or similar) are preferred for head and main studs.
Through-Bolt vs. Dilavar Stud Debate
The original Porsche design uses Dilavar studs to attach the main bearing webs. Dilavar's low thermal expansion rate
matches the magnesium case, preventing bearing clearance changes with temperature. However, through-bolt kits (long
bolts that clamp the entire case from side to side) offer superior clamping force and are preferred for high-power
applications. For stock or mildly tuned street engines, Dilavar studs with proper torque are entirely adequate. For
engines making over 250 HP or seeing sustained high RPM, through-bolt conversion kits from manufacturers like
Raceware are good insurance.
Oil and Lubrication
During assembly, coat all bearing surfaces with assembly lube (Lubriplate, Clevite Bearing Guard, or equivalent). Pre-fill
the oil pump. Use new O-rings on all oil passages. Use a quality oil filter (Mahle OC49 or equivalent, not the cheapest
aftermarket option available). The air-cooled engine runs hotter than water-cooled designs and depends entirely on oil
quality for bearing protection.
Final Words
An air-cooled Porsche engine rebuild is a deeply rewarding project for the skilled home mechanic. Take your time,
measure everything twice, and do not cut corners on parts quality. A properly rebuilt engine will deliver another
100,000+ miles of reliable service. If in doubt about any measurement or procedure, consult a specialist -- this is not the
place to guess.

Chapter 11: Transmission Rebuild Guide
