Overview of Porsche Air-Cooled Transmissions
Porsche used three primary transmission families in the air-cooled 911 lineage. Understanding which one you have is
the first step before ordering parts or planning a rebuild.
901/902 Transmission (1965-1971)
The 901 is a 5-speed fully synchronized transaxle introduced with the original 911. The 902 is the 4-speed variant used
in the 912. These are side-shift transmissions with the shift linkage on the driver's side of the case. The gear pattern is a
dogleg: first gear is down-and-left, with second through fifth in an H-pattern.
The 901 is a generally reliable unit, but it was designed for the 130 HP 2.0L engine and becomes marginal when
subjected to more than about 180 HP. The synchros are brass, the gears are straight-cut (first) and helical (second
through fifth), and the ring-and-pinion is a hypoid design located in an aluminum differential housing bolted to the main
gearbox case.
Key 901 weakness: the limited-slip differential (when fitted) uses a ZF clutch-pack design that wears and needs periodic
rebuild. The standard open differential is essentially bulletproof for street use.
915 Transmission (1972-1986)
The 915 replaced the 901 and was used for the longest span of any air-cooled Porsche transmission. It was introduced
with the 2.4L engines in 1972 and continued through the end of 3.2 Carrera production in 1986 (with updates along the
way).
The 915 is also a side-shift design. Early 915 units (1972-1974) have an aluminum case and are sometimes called
"short nose" due to the input shaft length. Starting in 1976, the case was updated with improved bearing support.
The 915's well-known weakness is second gear synchro wear. The synchro cone angle and surface area for second
gear are marginally sized for the loads involved, especially in spirited driving. Symptoms include a grinding or crunching
feel when shifting from third to second, particularly when the gearbox is cold. Many owners learn to double-clutch into
second, which is good driving practice regardless, but a worn synchro should be replaced.
Page 71

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Other 915 issues include:
  -  Shift rod detent springs weakening, causing the transmission to pop out of fifth gear under deceleration.
  -  Input shaft bearing failure, presenting as a whine that changes with clutch engagement/disengagement.
  -  Ring-and-pinion wear in high-mileage units, producing a howl on deceleration.
The 915 is a robust unit when properly rebuilt and is more than capable of handling the output of a stock 3.2 Carrera
engine (231 HP).
G50 Transmission (1987-1998)
The G50 was a clean-sheet Getrag/Porsche design introduced with the 1987 Carrera 3.2. It moved to a cable-shifted
mechanism and Borg-Warner synchro design with a dramatically improved shift feel. The shift pattern is conventional
(no dogleg).
Key design differences from the 915:
  -  Cable shift replaces the rod/coupler side-shift mechanism
  -  Larger synchro cone area virtually eliminates second-gear synchro issues
  -  Beefier bearings throughout
  -  Hydraulic clutch actuation replaces the cable clutch (on most models)
  -  Dual-mass flywheel on some applications (964, 993)
The G50 is the most reliable of the three transmission families for street use. Common failures are limited to clutch
release bearing wear (a whirring noise with the clutch pedal depressed) and cable adjustment issues. The G50/50
variant used in the 993 is the strongest of the line.
Disassembly Overview
Transmission rebuilding requires cleanliness, patience, and organization. Work on a clean, well-lit bench. Have a factory
workshop manual specific to your transmission type -- the 915 and G50 have very different internal architectures.
General procedure:
1. Drain gear oil completely. Note its condition: metal particles indicate gear or bearing wear; brass particles indicate
synchro wear.
2. Remove the transmission from the car (support the engine independently).
3. Separate the bell housing from the main case.
4. Remove the shift mechanism (side cover on 901/915, cables on G50).
5. Remove the rear cover/differential housing.
6. Remove the circlips and bearings that retain the mainshaft and pinion shaft in the case.
7. Lift out the gear clusters as an assembly.
8. Separate the differential from the ring gear if ring-and-pinion replacement is needed.
Page 72

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
9. Disassemble gear clusters on the bench, noting the order and orientation of every synchro hub, sleeve, blocker
ring, and thrust washer.
Critical: Use a press for bearing removal and installation. Hammering bearings onto shafts damages them instantly.
Heat the case to 100C (212F) to ease bearing installation into the housing bores.
Synchro Inspection and Replacement
Synchro blocker rings are the primary wear items in any Porsche transmission. Inspect by placing the blocker ring on its
matching gear cone and measuring the gap between the ring face and the gear shoulder. This is the "synchro gap."
Minimum synchro gap (all types):
  -  New: 1.5-2.0mm
  -  Wear limit: 0.8mm (901/915), 1.0mm (G50)
Below the wear limit, the ring cannot generate enough friction to equalize shaft speeds, and grinding occurs. Replace
synchro rings as a set per gear -- never replace just one ring from a double-cone synchro assembly.
Synchro ring suppliers:
  -  OEM Porsche (expensive but guaranteed correct)
  -  Wevo (aftermarket, good reputation for 915 synchros)
  -  Genuine Borg-Warner for G50 units
On the 915, the second gear synchro is almost always the most worn. Many rebuilders upgrade to an aftermarket
second gear synchro kit with increased cone area. The Wevo 2nd gear synchro kit is widely regarded as a meaningful
improvement.
Bearing Replacement
Replace all mainshaft, pinion shaft, and differential bearings during a rebuild regardless of apparent condition. These are
the bearings that control gear mesh alignment -- worn bearings allow shaft deflection that accelerates gear tooth wear.
Common bearing sizes (verify against your specific transmission):
915 Transmission:
  -  Input shaft front bearing: 6306 (30x72x19mm)
  -  Input shaft rear bearing: 6207 (35x72x17mm)
  -  Pinion shaft front bearing: 6305 (25x62x17mm)
  -  Pinion shaft rear bearing: 6206 (30x62x16mm)
  -  Differential bearings: tapered roller, matched set
Page 73

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
G50 Transmission:
  -  Uses tapered roller bearings throughout the mainshaft and pinion shaft for improved rigidity. These must be properly
preloaded during assembly.
Use only name-brand bearings: SKF, FAG, or INA. Cheap offshore bearings in a Porsche transaxle are a false economy
that will cost you the entire rebuild when they fail prematurely.
Input Shaft and Pinion Inspection
Examine gear teeth for pitting, chipping, and wear patterns. Minor polishing on the contact faces is normal. Chips on
tooth edges indicate shock loading or foreign object damage.
Check the input shaft splines (where the clutch disc engages) for wear. A loose, sloppy fit on the splines causes clutch
chatter. The shaft can be replaced individually if the splines are worn, but if the gears are integral to the shaft, the entire
shaft assembly must be replaced.
The pinion gear and ring gear are a matched set -- always replace both if either is damaged. New ring-and-pinion sets
must have backlash and contact pattern checked during assembly. Target backlash for most Porsche transaxles is
0.10-0.15mm (0.004-0.006 in). Verify contact pattern with Prussian blue or gear marking compound.
Gear Oil Specifications
This is critical and frequently gotten wrong.
All air-cooled Porsche transmissions with brass synchro rings (901, 915, and G50) require GL-4 rated gear oil ONLY.
Never use GL-5 oil. GL-5 contains extreme-pressure (EP) additives with sulfur-phosphorus compounds that are
chemically aggressive to yellow metals (brass, bronze, copper). GL-5 oil will dissolve your synchro rings over a period of
several thousand miles, creating exactly the grinding problem you are trying to prevent.
Recommended oils:
  -  Porsche factory fill: SAE 75W-90 GL-4 (cold climate) or 80W-90 GL-4 (warm climate)
  -  Swepco 201: a popular choice among Porsche specialists, GL-4, excellent synchro compatibility
  -  Redline MT-90: 75W-90 GL-4, widely available
  -  Motul Gear 300: 75W-90 GL-4+, fully synthetic
Capacity:
  -  901: approximately 2.5 liters
  -  915: approximately 3.0 liters (fill to the bottom of the filler hole with the car level)
  -  G50: approximately 3.0-3.25 liters
Change interval: every 30,000 miles or 3 years.
Page 74

Differential Rebuild
The differential is housed in its own aluminum carrier bolted to the rear of the transmission case. The open differential
requires little attention beyond bearing replacement. If you have a limited-slip differential (LSD), the clutch plates and
Belleville spring washer will need inspection and likely replacement.
LSD specifications:
  -  901/915 ZF LSD: 40% lock under load (when new). Rebuild kits available from Quaife, Wavetrac, and OEM Porsche.
  -  G50 LSD: similar clutch-pack design, 20-40% lock depending on application.
When assembling the differential carrier, set the ring gear bolt torque to 75-85 Nm (55-63 ft-lbs) with blue Loctite 242 on
the threads. Set differential bearing preload by shimming until 0.05-0.10mm (0.002-0.004 in) of preload is achieved
(slight drag when rotating the assembly by hand with no gears meshed).
Shift Fork Adjustment
Worn shift forks allow incomplete gear engagement, leading to gear pop-out and, eventually, broken gear teeth. Inspect
the shift fork pads (the contact surfaces that push the synchro sleeves). Wear grooves deeper than 0.5mm require fork
replacement.
On the 915, shift fork alignment is adjusted by shimming the shift rod positions within the case. The shift rods must move
freely without binding. Detent ball springs must be strong enough to hold each gear position firmly but not so stiff that
shifting becomes heavy.
On the G50, shift fork adjustment is less complex due to the cable-shift design, but the cable free play must be correctly
set at the transmission end to ensure full engagement travel.
Clutch: Cable vs. Hydraulic
Cable Clutch (911 through 1986)
The cable clutch system uses a Bowden cable running from the pedal to a release lever on the transmission. Cable free
play must be maintained at 15-20mm measured at the release lever. Adjust at the cable clevis on the lever. A cable with
no free play will ride on the release bearing and destroy it. A cable with too much play will not fully disengage the clutch.
Replace the cable if it is frayed, stretched, or if the housing is cracked. Route the new cable carefully to avoid kinks.
Lubricate annually with cable lube or light oil injected at the pedal end.
Clutch pedal free play at the pedal: approximately 25-30mm before resistance is felt.
Page 75

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
Hydraulic Clutch (G50, 1987 onward)
The G50 uses a hydraulic master and slave cylinder. Self-adjusting by design -- no routine adjustment needed. Flush
the hydraulic fluid (DOT 4 brake fluid) every 2 years to prevent moisture contamination, which causes internal corrosion
and eventual failure of the slave cylinder seal.
Bleed the clutch hydraulics using the same procedure as brake bleeding: crack the bleeder valve on the slave cylinder,
have an assistant press the pedal, close the valve, release the pedal, repeat until no bubbles appear.
Common failure: the clutch slave cylinder internal seal hardens and leaks after 15-20 years. Replace prophylactically
during any transmission-out service.
Clutch Disc and Pressure Plate
For all transmissions, inspect the clutch disc friction surface for wear. Minimum disc thickness is typically 7.0mm (new is
approximately 8.5mm). Check the pressure plate fingers for even height (no more than 0.5mm variation). Replace the
release bearing at every clutch service -- it is a $20-40 part that requires transmission removal to access.
Clutch disc bolt torque (pressure plate to flywheel): 25-30 Nm (18-22 ft-lbs), tighten in a star pattern in stages.

Final Notes
A transmission rebuild is more intimidating than an engine rebuild for most home mechanics, largely because the
internal relationships between gear positions, synchro assemblies, and selector mechanisms are less intuitive than
pistons and valves. The key is organization and reference. Take extensive photographs during disassembly. Lay out
Page 76

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
every component in order on a clean bench. Use the factory workshop manual for your specific transmission variant --
the differences between a 1972 915 and a 1986 915 are significant enough that generic instructions can lead you astray.
If you are rebuilding a 915 and want to improve the shift quality beyond stock, consider the Wevo shift kit, which
replaces the factory shift coupler and detent mechanism with a much tighter, more precise system. For G50 owners, the
transmission is generally so good from the factory that aftermarket modification is unnecessary unless you are pursuing
motorsport.
A properly rebuilt Porsche transaxle is incredibly durable. Treat it to the correct oil, adjust the clutch properly, and it will
outlast the engine between rebuilds.

Porsche 356 Technical Guide
Model History
The Porsche 356 was produced from 1948 to 1965 and established Porsche as a sports car manufacturer. Based on
Volkswagen mechanicals but refined into a true sports car, the 356 evolved through four major series.
Pre-A (1948-1955)
The earliest production Porsches. Split-windshield coupes gave way to bent-windshield models around 1952. Engine
displaced 1086cc (40 HP) in the earliest cars, growing to 1488cc and 1582cc. Bodies were handcrafted in Gmund,
Austria initially, then moved to Stuttgart-Zuffenhausen. These cars are the most collectible but also the most challenging
to maintain due to unique early parts.
356A (1955-1959)
The first major redesign. Curved one-piece windshield, improved interior, and the definitive 356 shape. Available as
1300, 1300 Super, 1600, 1600 Super, and the legendary Carrera with the Type 547 four-cam engine. The 356A
introduced the T1 and T2 body variations. Approximately 21,000 produced.
356B (1959-1963)
Raised bumpers and headlights distinguish the B from the A. Available in T5 and T6 body styles. The T6 introduced a
larger rear window and an external fuel filler. Engine options included the 1600 (60 HP), Super 90 (90 HP), and Carrera
2 with the 2.0-liter Type 587 four-cam engine producing 130 HP. Twin-grille engine lid on T6 models.
356C (1963-1965)
The final evolution. Most significant change: disc brakes on all four wheels replaced the drums used on all earlier 356s.
Only two engine options: the 1600 C (75 HP) and 1600 SC (95 HP). The SC engine is considered the best of the
pushrod 356 engines. Approximately 16,700 produced before the 356 gave way to the 911 in 1965.

6-Volt System
All 356s from 1948 through 1965 used a 6-volt electrical system. This is adequate for the original equipment but

One of the most popular 356 modifications. Benefits include brighter headlights, easier starting, and compatibility with

Requirements for 12-volt conversion:
1. 12-volt battery (smaller group size fits the battery box)
2. 12-volt generator or alternator conversion
3. 12-volt voltage regulator
4. Replace all light bulbs with 12-volt equivalents
5. Replace wiper motor (or install resistor for some models)
6. Replace ignition coil with 12-volt coil

Brake Systems
Drum Brakes (Pre-A through 356B)
All 356 models through the B series used drum brakes. These are adequate for the car's weight and period performance
but require more maintenance than discs.
  -  Front drums: 280mm diameter, 40mm wide
  -  Rear drums: 280mm diameter, 40mm wide
  -  Lining material: organic or semi-metallic
  -  Adjustment: star wheel at backing plate
  -  Drums should be turned if scored (minimum thickness stamped on drum)
Common drum brake issues:
  -  Fading under repeated hard use (overheating)
  -  Pulling to one side (oil on linings, stuck wheel cylinder)
  -  Spongy pedal (need bleeding or wheel cylinder leaking)
Disc Brakes (356C Only)
The 356C was the first 356 with four-wheel disc brakes, using Dunlop-type solid rotors and single-piston calipers.
  -  Front rotor: 282mm x 12.7mm (minimum 11.5mm)
  -  Rear rotor: 285mm x 12.7mm (minimum 11.5mm)
  -  Caliper: single piston, Annette type
  -  Brake fluid: DOT 4
Suspension
Front Suspension
The 356 uses a VW-derived front suspension with two laminated torsion bars in a front cross-tube, trailing arms, and link
pins (early) or ball joints (late 356B and 356C).
  -  Link pin front end: requires periodic greasing and adjustment. Wear in the link pins causes vague steering and
shimmy.
  -  Ball joint front end (from 1963): much improved, similar to later VW and Karmann Ghia. Less maintenance required.
Page 81

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
  -  Front torsion bars: 2 leaves early, multiple leaves later
  -  Shock absorbers: telescopic on later cars, lever-type on earliest models
Rear Suspension
Swing axle rear suspension with torsion bars. The swing axle design means significant camber change during
suspension travel, which can cause unpredictable handling at the limit (the infamous "snap oversteer").
  -  Rear torsion bars: located in tubes running across the chassis
  -  Spring plate angle determines ride height
  -  Shock absorbers: telescopic
  -  Compensating spring (Z-bar) added on later models to reduce swing axle jacking
Common 356 Problems and Solutions
1. Generator not charging: Check brushes, field coil, voltage regulator. VW generators are interchangeable with
some bracket modification.
2. Oil leaks at pushrod tubes: The rubber seals (O-rings) at the pushrod tube ends deteriorate. Replace with silicone
seals. Some owners use the later 911-style spring-loaded pushrod tubes for a permanent fix.
3. Heater box deterioration: The heat exchangers rust from inside out due to condensation. Replacement boxes are
available but expensive. Inspect yearly.
4. Fuel pump failure: Mechanical fuel pump driven off the engine. Rebuild kits are available. Carry a spare pump on
long trips.
5. Transmission noise: First and second gear whine is common and usually not a failure. The crash-box
(non-synchro) first gear on early cars requires double-clutching.
6. Rust: 356s rust in the battery box area, front trunk floor, longitudinals, rocker panels, and around the rear window.
Any 356 purchase should include a thorough rust inspection.
356 vs Early 911 Parts Interchangeability
Some parts are shared or interchangeable between late 356 and early 911:
  -  Wheel bearings (front): 356C and early 911 share front wheel bearings
  -  Brake fluid reservoir: some models share the same reservoir
  -  Fuel pump: not directly interchangeable but similar mounting
  -  Electrical components: switches, relays vary but some overlap
  -  Wheels: 356C 15" wheels fit early 911s (same 5x130mm bolt pattern)
  -  Engine: the 356 engine is physically smaller and does NOT interchange with the 911 flat-six

Turbocharger System - How It Works
Single-Turbo System (930)
1. Exhaust gases from all six cylinders collect in the exhaust manifold
2. Hot exhaust drives the turbine wheel of the KKK K27 turbo
3. Turbine wheel spins the compressor wheel via a shared shaft
4. Compressor draws ambient air, compresses it
5. Compressed air passes through the intercooler (1978+) to reduce temperature
6. Cooled, compressed air enters the intake manifold
7. Wastegate regulates maximum boost by bypassing exhaust around the turbine
Twin-Turbo System (993)
Same principle but with two smaller turbochargers, one per cylinder bank:
Page 93

Porsche Air-Cooled Technical Owners Book | Reno Rennsport 2026
  -  Left turbo serves cylinders 1-2-3
  -  Right turbo serves cylinders 4-5-6
  -  Each has its own wastegate and intercooler
  -  Smaller turbos spool faster = less turbo lag
  -  More even exhaust flow = smoother power delivery
Turbo Lag
The infamous 930 Turbo lag occurs because the large single K27 turbocharger needs time to spool up. At low RPM
there is minimal boost. When boost arrives (typically above 3,500 RPM), it arrives suddenly, adding 40-50% more power
in an instant.
Managing Turbo Lag
  -  Keep the engine above 3,000 RPM when expecting to need power
  -  Downshift before corners so boost is available on exit
  -  In the wet, be EXTREMELY cautious - sudden boost on a wet road with the engine behind the rear axle is the classic
930 crash scenario
  -  The 993 twin-turbo largely eliminated this problem with its smaller, faster-spooling turbos
Wastegate and Boost Control
Mechanical Wastegate (930)
  -  Spring-loaded valve in the exhaust housing
  -  Opens at preset boost level (0.8 bar) to bypass exhaust around turbine
  -  Not adjustable without changing the spring
  -  Aftermarket adjustable wastegate actuators available (increases boost but increases risk)
Electronic Boost Control (993 Turbo)
  -  ECU-controlled wastegate solenoid

Common Turbo-Specific Problems
1. Wastegate Failure
Symptoms: Overboosting (boost exceeds 0.8 bar), boost surging, check engine light (993).
Cause: Wastegate actuator diaphragm tear, stuck wastegate valve.
Fix: Replace wastegate actuator. On 930, inspect the frequency valve (CIS cars).
2. Turbo Oil Seal Leak
Symptoms: Blue smoke on startup or deceleration, oil in intercooler piping.
Cause: Worn turbo shaft seals allowing oil into the compressor or turbine housing.
Fix: Turbo rebuild or replacement. Inspect oil supply and drain lines for restriction.
3. Boost Leak
Symptoms: Low power, turbo spools but no power increase, hissing sound under boost.
Cause: Cracked or disconnected boost hoses, intercooler pipe connections, failed pressure lines.
Fix: Pressurize the intake system with a boost leak tester. Check all silicone couplers and clamps.
4. Cracked Exhaust Manifold
Symptoms: Ticking noise that increases with RPM, exhaust smell, reduced boost.
Cause: Thermal cycling cracks the cast iron manifold, especially on the 930.
Fix: Weld repair (temporary) or replace manifold. Aftermarket stainless steel headers are more durable.
5. Oil Coking in Turbo Bearings
Symptoms: Turbo whine, reduced boost, eventual turbo failure.

Fix: Prevention - always idle for 2-3 minutes after spirited driving. Aftermarket turbo timer or oil accumulator helps.

Oil Recommendations for Turbo Models
Turbo engines run significantly hotter than naturally aspirated engines. Oil selection is critical:
  -  Recommended: Mobil 1 15W-50 or 0W-40 full synthetic
  -  Alternative: Motul 300V 15W-50, Brad Penn 20W-50
  -  NEVER use conventional oil in a turbo engine
  -  Change oil every 5,000 miles maximum (not the 7,500 mile interval of NA cars)
  -  Oil capacity is larger on Turbo models due to additional oil cooler
Performance Modifications
Common Turbo Upgrades
1. Boost increase: Adjustable wastegate actuator or ECU tune. Going from 0.8 to 1.0 bar adds approximately 30-40
HP but increases stress on all components.
2. Exhaust: Free-flowing exhaust reduces backpressure, helps turbo spool faster.
3. Intercooler upgrade: Larger or more efficient intercooler reduces intake temps.
4. Fuel system: Larger injectors and fuel pump required above ~350 HP on 930.
5. Clutch: Upgraded pressure plate and disc for higher torque applications.
Warning on Modifications
The 930 engine internals (particularly the crankcase and rod bolts) were designed for 300 HP. Pushing significantly
beyond this without strengthening internals risks catastrophic failure. The 993 Turbo is more robust but should not
exceed 500 HP without engine reinforcement.

Turbo Driving Tips
1. Warm up thoroughly before boosting - oil temperature should reach 180F minimum
2. Never lug the engine in high gear and floor it - this creates maximum cylinder pressure at low RPM (detonation
risk)
3. After highway driving or spirited use, idle for 2-3 minutes before shutdown
4. In the rain, respect the turbo lag - 930s are particularly dangerous in wet conditions due to sudden power delivery
and rear weight bias
5. Check boost gauge regularly - a sudden change in max boost indicates a problem
